coredata.dc:title: New nanosensor fabricated on single nanopore electrode filled with prussian blue and graphene quantum dots coated by polypyrrole for hydrogen peroxide sensing
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Xiaohu
Dai, Qingshan
Qiu, Xia
Luo, Xianzhun
Li, Yongxin
coredata.dc:description: Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) is a common oxidant that plays an important role in many biological processes and is also an important medium analysis in various fields. In this work, a new electrochemical nanosensor capable of detecting and quantifying hydrogen peroxide was introduced. This nanosensor was fabricated by electrodepositing prussian blue (PB)/graphene quantum dots (GQDs)/polypyrrole (PPy) on single nanopore electrode etched from single gold nanoelectrode. This prepapred nanosensor exhibits good electrochemical response to hydrogen peroxide with high sensitivity and stability, with a linear response in the 2.0 and 80 μM by using amperometric method and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) method. The limit of detections are 0.33 μM (S/N = 3) for amperometric method and 0.67 μM (S/N = 3) for differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) method, respectively. This nanosensor can be used for the determination of hydrogen peroxide in human urine, and can serve as a new electrochemical platform to monitor H2O2 release from single living cells due to its small overal dimension and high sensitivity.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Hydrogen peroxide sensing by using PPy/GQDs/PB/SAuNPE

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Anhui Provincial Department of Education Scientific Research Project
National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China
Natural Science Foundation of Anhui Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No.21775003), Anhui Provincial Natural Science Foundation (No. 2308085MB56), and the Key Project of Anhui Provincial Department of Education Scientific Research Project (2023AH040032)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of the GQDs (B) The UV of the GQDs, (C) HRTEM of single gold nanoelectrode sealed by glass tube (D) SEM image of single gold nanopore electrode.

Steady-state voltammetric responses of gold nanoelectrode (black line) and etched gold nanopore electrode (red line) in (A) 5 mM Fc ACN solution containing 0.1 M TBAPF6, (B) 5 mM Ru(NH3)6Cl3 containing 0.1 M KCl, (C) 5 mM K3Fe(CN)6 containing 0.1 M KCl, (D) the simulation result of Fc diffusion on the surface of Au nanoelectrodes and Au nanopore electrode. The scan rate is 50 mV/s.

(A) Votammetric responses using (a) SAuNPE, (b) PB/SAuNPE, (c) PPy/PB/SAuNPE and (d) PPy/GQDs/PB/SAuNPE in 0.5 M KCl solution (a); (B) Votammetric responses using PPy/GQDs/PB/SAuNPE in 0.5 M KCl solution without (black line) and with (red line) 0.1 mM H2O2. Scan rate: 50 mV/s; Radius of SAuNPE, ∼120 nm. Depth of SAuNPE, ∼500 nm.

(A) DPV of different concentrations of H2O2 at and PPy/GQDs/PB/SAuNPE immersed in 0.5 M KCl, the concentration of H2O2 from 2 to 80 μm; (B) calibration plot from the chronoamperogram (R2 = 0.9947). Scan rate: 50 mV/s; Radius of SAuNPE, ∼120 nm. Depth of SAuNPE, ∼500 nm.

(A) Typical i-t curve of different concentrations of H2O2 at and PPy/GQDs/PB/SAuNPE immersed in 0.5 M KCl, the concentration of H2O2 from 2 to 80 μm; (B) calibration plot from the chronoamperogram (R2 = 0.9936). Scan rate: 50 mV/s; Radius of SAuNPE, ∼120 nm. Depth of SAuNPE, ∼500 nm.

(A) Amperometeric responses for the detection of 5 μM (a) H2O2 in the presence of 50 μM (b) Na+, 50 μM (c) Cl−, (d) 50 μM ascorbic acid, (e) 50 μM uric acid, (f) 50 μM quinol, (g) 50 μM acetamide, (h) 50 μM glucose, (i) 50 μM urea, (j) 50 μM cystein, (k) 50 μM dopamine. (B) Continuous seven days testing Amperometric reaction of 5 μM H2O2 (B); Radius of SAuNPE, ∼120 nm; Depth of SAuNPE, ∼500 nm.
PPy/GQDs/PB films were formed on the surface of the SAuNPEs by depositing PB on the surface of the electrode and then continuing to modify PPy and GQDs.
Performance Comparison of PPy/GQDs/PB/SAuNPE nanosensors for H2O2 sensing and other reported methods.
Determination of H2O2 in spiked human urine samples.

head.ce:title: New nanosensor fabricated on single nanopore electrode filled with prussian blue and graphene quantum dots coated by polypyrrole for hydrogen peroxide sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Anhui Province Key Laboratory of Biomedical Materials and Chemical Measurement, Key Laboratory of Functional Molecular Solids, Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Anhui Normal University, Wuhu, 241000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Anhui Province Key Laboratory of Biomedical Materials and Chemical Measurement
Key Laboratory of Functional Molecular Solids
College of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Anhui Province Key Laboratory of Biomedical Materials and Chemical Measurement, Key Laboratory of Functional Molecular Solids, Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Anhui Normal University, Wuhu, 241000, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) is a common oxidant that plays an important role in many biological processes and is also an important medium analysis in various fields. In this work, a new electrochemical nanosensor capable of detecting and quantifying hydrogen peroxide was introduced. This nanosensor was fabricated by electrodepositing prussian blue (PB)/graphene quantum dots (GQDs)/polypyrrole (PPy) on single nanopore electrode etched from single gold nanoelectrode. This prepapred nanosensor exhibits good electrochemical response to hydrogen peroxide with high sensitivity and stability, with a linear response in the 2.0 and 80 μM by using amperometric method and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) method. The limit of detections are 0.33 μM (S/N = 3) for amperometric method and 0.67 μM (S/N = 3) for differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) method, respectively. This nanosensor can be used for the determination of hydrogen peroxide in human urine, and can serve as a new electrochemical platform to monitor H2O2 release from single living cells due to its small overal dimension and high sensitivity.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Because of the small overall dimension, low background current, high current density and fast mass transfer rate, nanoelectrodes have been widely used in electrochemical fundamental research and practice applications [1–4]. For example, it can be used as a probe for electrochemical imaging analysis [5], live cell detection [6], single cell analysis [7], and so on. Especially, nanoelectrodes have unique advantages in detecting analytes in confined space, such as tissue analysis and single cell detection [8,9]. However, low current response from nanoelectrode is always a problem for practice application due to the tiny size of nanoelectrodes [10]. To solve this problem, several strategies were developed, such as nanowire electrodes and nanoelectrode arrays [11,12], signal amplification technique [13], or electrodeposition method [14]. Therefore, it is still a strategy to develop novel nanosensors with high sensitivity for real applications (see Scheme 1 ).

Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) is a common oxidant that plays an important role in many biological processes. In addition, it is also an important media analysis for biomedicine, pharmaceutical, clinical, enzymatic reaction, and environmental aspects [15–17]. Therefore, the reliable, sensitive, and rapid determination of H2O2 has important practical significance. So far, various analytical methods have been proposed for the quantitative detection of H2O2, such as titration [18,19], colorimetry [20–22], electrochemistry [23,24], chemiluminescence, etc. [25] Among the above methods, electrochemical technology has been widely used for the determination of H2O2 due to its high accuracy. A large number of electrochemical biosensors have been fabricated for the electrocatalytic effect of H2O2 reduction. Schuhmann's team reported a H2O2 nanosensor on the platform of Prussian blue modified nanoelectrodes [8,26]. The limitations of existing PB based H2O2 sensors are low signal response and easy detachment of PB deposited on the surface. For practical applications of nanosensors, there are certain limitations.

In this work, a new nanosensor was fabricated on single gold nanopore electrode (SAuNPE), which was etched from single gold nanoelectrode (SAuNE) prepared by laser-assisted pulling method [27–29]. Insided the nanopore electrode, prussian Blue (PB) was electrodeposited in the pores [30], and then graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and polypyrrole (PPy) were deposited into the PB-modified nanopore (see Scheme 1) [31,32]. As the commonly electrochemical mediators, PB with favorable biocompatibility and unique properties was widely used in the biosensor field, but the instability of PB under alkaline conditions limited its application. Moreover, PPy was one of the common conductive polymers and GQDs had excellent physical and chemical properties, high stability, biocompatibility, small size and simple synthesis. Therefore, PB, GQDs and PPy are used to modify single gold nanopore electrode to achieve high sensitivity for the determination of trace H2O2.

Gold wire with the diameter of 25 μm and tungsten wire with the diameter of 250 μm were obtained from Alfa Aesar. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China), Ferrocene (Fc, Fluka), acetonitrile (ACN, Shanghai Bellinvik), Ruthenium (III) trichlorohexamide (Ru(NH3)6Cl3, Aldrich), potassium ferricyanide (K3Fe(CN)6, Aldrich), Potassium chloride (KCl), dopamine (DA), uric acid (UA), ascorbic acid (AA), prussian blue (PB), and pyrrole (Py) were supplied by Sangon Biotech Company (Shanghai, China). All chemical materials are analytically pure grade and can be used in experiments without further purification. Ultrapure water is obtained from the Millipore water purification system (18.25 MΩ cm) and is used to prepare all aqueous solutions.

Gold nanoelectrodes (Au NEs) were fabricated through pulling goldwire/capillaries with a puller (P-2000 mode, Sutter Company). TEM images were takens from a microscope (Hitachi-7700 mode, Tokyo, Japan). UV–vis spectra was obtained from a 3000-mode UV–vis spectrophotometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific Evolution, USA). An electrochemical workstation (CHI900C mode, Chenhua, China) was used to perform electrochemical experiments. High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) is used to capture NE (G2 F20 S-TWIN, FEI CO., USA) An Ag/AgCl electrode was used as the reference electrode and a platinum wire was used as auxiliary electrode.

The Au NEs were prepared according to the method previously reported in our laboratory [28,33], and the preparation process mainly consists of four steps. First, a gold wire was inserted into a capillary tube. Second, the gold wire-inserted capillary was then pulled using the Sutter P-2000 laser pipette puller as follows:
Program
1 : Heat = 360 , Filament = 4 , Velocity = 25 , Delay = 225 , Pull = 118
Program
2 : Heat = 400 , Filament = 1 , Velocity = 30 , Delay = 120 , Pull = 120

Program 1 includes 3 cycles each cycle is composed of a 45-s heating cycle and a 15-s cooling cycle. Proceed to program 2 immediately after program 1 to get two sharp tips. Third, check whether the drawn gold wire is continuously available with an optical microscope, and then seal the borosilicate glass tube with epoxy resin. The tungsten wire and the gold wire were connected with silver conductive glue to obtain the gold nanoelectrode. Finally, under the monitoring of an optical microscope, the needle tip was ground with different sandpaper to obtain a single gold nanoelectrode.

Then, the sealed AuNE was electrochemically etched by 20s linear sweep voltammetry (i-t) at 1.5 V potential in 15% CaCl2 solution to form gold nanopore electrode [29]. After the formation of pore, rinse with water and ethanol three times to keep the pore clean. Afterwards, a constant potential of +400 mV (vs. Ag/AgCl) was applied to 2.5 mM FeCl3 and 2.5 mM K3Fe(CN)6 for 300 s in a 0.1 M HCl+0.1 M KCl solution. After deposition, the obtained NE was activated at a scanning rate of 50 mV/s from −0.2 V to 0.5 V for 25 cycles, represented by PB/SAuNPE [26,30].

The process for preparing PPy/GQDs modified PB/SAuNPE is to disperse GQDs in a 0.1 M SDS solution, sonicate for 30 min, and then dissolve pyrrole in the aforementioned solution for 20 min under sonication stirring. PB/SauNE was immersed into the mixed solution, and performed four rounds of CV scanning between −0.2 V and 0.8 V, and the obtained modified NE was denoted as PPy/GQDs/PB/SAuNPE [34]. For comparison, only PPy was electrodeposited on PB/SAuNPE with the same CV method for PPy/GQDs deposition, which was denoted as PPy/PB/SAuNPE.

The synthesized GQDs were characterized by TEM and UV shown in Fig. 1 A and B. From Fig. 1A, we can see that GQDs are spherical and evenly distributed, with a size of ∼8 nm and Fig. 1B shows that the UV peak of GQDs is around 350 nm, which is in accordance with previous report [35]. The morphologies of the prepared SAuNEs and SAuNPEs were provided Fig. 1C and D. The transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) result of SAuNE was given in Fig. 1C, which showed that the gold nanowire was sealed inside the glass tube tightly without obvious gap. The scanning electron microscopy (SEM) result of SAuNPEs was provided in Fig. 1D, showing that a perfect pore was formed on the disk electrode with a diameter of ∼100 nm.

Furthermore, SAuNEs and SAuNPEs were further characterized by the electrochemical method. Fig. 2 recorded the CVs by use of a single gold nano-disk electrode (black curve) and a nanopore electrode (red curve) in Fc ACN solution containing 0.1 M TBAPF6 (A), a 5 mM Ru(NH3)6Cl3 containing 0.1 M KCl (B) and a 5 mM K3Fe(CN)6 containing 0.1 M KCl (C). Fig. 2A, B and 2C show that all the CVs have well-defined “S” shape with low charge current, indicating that the single gold nanoelectrode and nanopore electrodes were fabricated successfully. The radii of gold nanoelectrode can be estimated from the following equation (1). After etching, the depth of the nanopore electrode can be calculated from equation (2) or COMSOL simulation (shown in Fig. 2D, Fig. S1and Fig. S2). calculation result shows that the radius of a single gold nanoelectrode is 120 nm, and the depth of the gold nanopore electrode is 500 nm [12,28].
(1)
i d
= 4 n F D
C b
a
(2)
i
p o r e
= 4 n F D
C b
a
[
π
4 d / a + π
]
where n is the number of electron-transferred per molecule, F is Faraday's constant, D is the diffusion coefficient, C
b is the bulk concentration of redox species, a is the radius of gold nanodisk electrode and d is the depth of nanopore electrode. From the limiting current shown in Fig. 2 and above equations, the radius and depth of the nanopore electrode can be calculated as 120 nm and 500 nm, respectively.

Next, in Fig. S1, we simulated the radius and depth of nanoelectrodes and nanopores in FC ACN solution using software, which is consistent with experimental results shown in Fig. 2. In Fig. S2, we further simulated the 2D model of the nanoelectrode radius and the 3D model of the gold nanopore electrode through computer simulations, further confirming the correctness of the calculation of radius and pore depth [29].

Fig. 3 A provides the CVs of SAuNPE (curve a), PB/SAuNPE (curve b), PPy/PB/SAuNPE (curve c) and PPy/GQDs/PB/SAuNPE (curve d) in KCl solution with the concentration of 0.5 M. In curve a, it can be observed that there is no peak of SAuNPE. Due to the redox process of PB, a pair of redox peaks will appear near 0.2 V for PB/SAuNPE (curve b) [31,32]. When PB/SAuNPE is modified with PPy, the redox peak at around 0.2 V increased, which may be due to the PPy conductivity (curve c). In addition, when PPy and GQDs composite films were deposited into the pore of PB/SAuNPE, the redox peak current became great significantly due to the excellent conductivity of GQDs, thus the deposition of PPy and GQDs would accelerate the electron transfer rate and increase the catalytic activity towards H2O2 electro-reduction. In addition, in Fig. S3, it can be observed that PPy/GQDs/PB/SAuNPE scans for 1, 25, and 50 cycles in a 0.1 M HCl+0.1 M KCl solution, with almost no change in current, on the contrary, as the number of scanning cycles increase, the current of GQDs/PB/SAuNPE decrease, which may be due to the excellent stability of PPy [12]. In Fig. S4, We can observe that GQDs are modified to the PB/SAuNPE.

In Fig. 3B, it can be observed that when the electrode is not added with 0.1 mM H2O2 (black line) and when 0.1 mM H2O2 is added (red line), the anodic peak current becomes much larger [36], while the cathodic peak current slightly decreases, indicating that H2O2 can catalyze the oxidation of PB modified on electrode, and the reaction process may be as following equation (3) and equation (4) [37]:
(3)
KFe
3 +
[
Fe
2 +
( CN )
6
]
+
e ‐
+

K 2

]
(4)
2
K 2

[
Fe

]
+
H 2
O 2
→
2
KFe
3 +
[
Fe
2 +
( CN )

]
+ 2
OH ‐
+ 2
K +

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hydrogen peroxide sensing by using PPy/GQDs/PB/SAuNPE
ce:section.ce:para: In order to detect H2O2, differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) was used to assess the nanosensor by using PPy/GQDs/PB/SAuNPE. Fig. 4 A shows that the intensity of the oxidation peak in the range of 2–80 μM increases with the increase of hydrogen peroxide concentration. In Fig. 4B, the linear relationship between catalytic current and H2O2 concentration can be found in equation I (pA) = 2.75 + 0.3901 (μM) (R2 = 0.9947). Base on the equation of limit of detection (LOD) LOD = 3Sb/m, where Sb is the standard deviation of the blank measurement (n = 20) and m is the slope of the calibration curve, thus the LOD can be obtained as 0.67 μM [38–40]. In the DPV test, we took 20 μM as the parallel measurement value to calculate its relative standard deviation, which represents the precision of the measurement value, the RSD value is 2.18%, and the calculation results demonstrate good measurement stability.

To further assess the H2O2 sensing by using PPy/GQDs/PB/SAuNPE, amperometric method was used in this work. Fig. 5 A shows the typical current response after continuous addition of H2O2 in 0.5 M KCl, which exhibits good amperometric response when continuously adding H2O2. The calibration diagram for H2O2 measurement is shown in Fig. 5B, there is a good linear relationship within the concentration range of ranging from 2 to 80 μM. The linear relationship between catalytic current and H2O2 concentration is: I (pA) = 5.62 + 9.55C (μM) (R2 = 0.9936). The LOD can be calculated to be 0.33 μM (3 σ). In the i-t test, we took 60 μM as the parallel measurement value to calculate its relative standard deviation, with an RSD value of 1.87%, the calculation results showed good measurement stability. The performance comparison between PPy/GQDs/PB/SAuNPE and other stategies from literatures is shown in Table 1 [36,41–46], which showed great sensitivity.

To test the selectivity of prepared PPy/GQDs/PB/SAuNPEs for H2O2 sensing, the amperometric method was used to detect potential interference. The concentration of interference is 10 times larger than H2O2. The results were provided in Fig. 6 A, which showed that almost no interferences from co-existing species, such as Na+ (b), Cl−(c), AA (d), UA (e), quinol (f), acetamide (g), glucose (h), urea (i), cystein (j), DA (k). Regularly inject injection each day 5 μM H2O2 is used to detect the stability of the nanoprobe through the current method (shown in Fig. 6B), which revealed that the current value is almost the same from the first day to the third day, and decreased slightly on the fourth day, then it gradually decreased on the seventh day, which indicates that the prepared nanosensor has good stability for hydrogen peroxide sensing.

To demonstrate the application of PPy/GQDs/PB nanoelectrodes in practical analysis, this nanoelectrode was used to detect H2O2 in real human urine samples. We obtained urine samples from local hospitals for centrifugation, and then added different concentrations of H2O2 to human urine samples in the experiment to test the reliability of our method. The H2O2 method in the above samples was detected using the amperometric method, and the results are provided in Table 2 . The RSD result is between 3.4% and 4.7%, and the sample recovery rate range is from 102.3% to 108.4%, indicating that the interfering substances in the sample are not affected by the detection of H2O2, and also proving that the proposed nanosensor can be used for the analysis of actual samples.

In this work, a new nanosensor for H2O2 sensing was developed by electrodeposition of PPy/GQDs/PB films on SAuNPE surface. The prepared nanosensor showed good sensitivity and stability for H2O2 detection. Due to the advantages of small size, fast electrochemical response and strong signal amplification, this nanosensor can serve as a powerful platform for H2O2 sensing, which may be applied for the research of H2O2 release from single living cells due to its small overal dimension and high sensitivity.
There are no conflicts to declare.

Xiaohu Chen: Conceptualization, Data curation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Qingshan Dai: Conceptualization, Data curation, Investigation. Xia Qiu: Conceptualization, Investigation. Xianzhun Luo: Data curation, Methodology. Yongxin Li: Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No.21775003), Anhui Provincial Natural Science Foundation (No. 2308085MB56), and the Key Project of Anhui Provincial Department of Education Scientific Research Project (2023AH040032)

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2024.126043.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Peroxide test strips detect added hydrogen peroxide in raw milk at levels affecting bacterial load
J. Food Protect.

Cell Metabol.
